title: Zechariah 11
subtitle: The Rejected Shepherd and the Worthless Shepherd

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — "I Shepherded the Flock Marked for Slaughter"

heb.paragraph: The staff named noʼam ('Favor' or 'Grace') represents God's gracious covenant with the nations on Israel's behalf. When the shepherd breaks this staff (11:10), he revokes the covenant that had restrained the nations from destroying Israel. The name connects to the theology of divine favor (chen) that permeates the prophetic tradition: God's benevolence toward Israel is not a permanent entitlement but a covenantal reality that can be withdrawn.

The second staff, chovelim ('Union' or 'Bonds'), represents the covenant bond between Judah and Israel. Its breaking (11:14) symbolizes the rupture of national unity — the division of the kingdom that historically occurred after Solomon (1 Kgs 12) but is here presented as an eschatological act of divine judgment against a people who reject their shepherd.

panels.ctx: Chapter 11 is the darkest passage in Zechariah — a prophetic sign-act in which the prophet assumes the role of a good shepherd who is ultimately rejected by the flock he tends. The chapter opens with a lament over the destruction of the land's glory (11:1-3), then shifts to God's command for the prophet to shepherd 'the flock marked for slaughter' (11:4). The flock is exploited by buyers, sellers, and shepherds who feel no compassion (11:5). The prophet-shepherd takes two staffs named 'Favor' and 'Union' and shepherds the flock, removing three unnamed shepherds in a single month (11:8). But the flock detests the shepherd, and he grows weary of them (11:8). In response, the shepherd abandons the flock (11:9), breaks the staff 'Favor' (revoking the covenant with the nations), and the oppressed who were watching recognize this as God's word (11:11). The sign-act dramatizes the tragic dynamic of divine grace offered, rejected, and withdrawn.

cross.note: Ezekiel's indictment of the shepherds who exploit the flock and God's pledge to shepherd His people directly provides the theological framework within which Zechariah's rejected shepherd narrative operates.

Jesus' self-identification as the 'good shepherd' who 'lays down his life for the sheep' fulfills and transforms Zechariah's rejected shepherd motif: where Zechariah's shepherd was rejected, Jesus' rejection leads to redemptive sacrifice.
Jeremiah's woe against 'the shepherds who are destroying and scattering the sheep' and God's promise to raise up faithful shepherds parallels the shepherd discourse in Zechariah 11.

notes.note: The wail over Lebanon's cedars, Bashan's oaks, and the Jordan's thicket is a prophetic lament over the destruction of the land's glory. These are not merely trees but symbols of national pride and security — and their fall signals comprehensive judgment.

The 'flock marked for slaughter' is Israel under exploitative leadership. The buyers, sellers, and shepherds represent the corrupt power structure that profits from the people's suffering. God's declaration that He 'will no longer have pity' (11:6) is the most severe judgment statement in Zechariah.

The prophet-shepherd's ministry with two staffs — Favor and Union — represents God's gracious engagement with His people. The removal of 'three shepherds in one month' is one of the most debated verses in the Old Testament; attempts to identify them with specific historical figures have produced no scholarly consensus.

The breaking of the staff 'Favor' is a prophetic enactment of covenant annulment: the protective arrangement that restrained the nations from attacking Israel is revoked. This is not arbitrary but responsive — the flock's rejection of the good shepherd triggers the withdrawal of divine protection.

Calvin read the rejected shepherd as a type of Christ, whose ministry was similarly rejected by the very people He came to save. The breaking of the staffs represents the consequences of rejection: divine favor withdrawn and national unity shattered. Calvin warned that the same dynamic operates in every age: communities that reject God's appointed shepherds forfeit the blessings those shepherds mediate.

The phrase 'particularly the oppressed of the flock' (laken ʼaniyyei hattsʼon) indicates that the true beneficiaries of the shepherd's care are the poor and marginalized within the community — those most vulnerable to exploitation by the corrupt leadership described in 11:5.

The identity of the 'three shepherds' removed in one month is uncertain. The NET notes survey the major proposals: three historical kings, three types of leaders (king, priest, prophet), or three representative oppressors. No identification has achieved scholarly consensus.

Boda reads chapter 11 as the 'anti-shepherd oracle,' which inverts the positive shepherd theology of chapter 10. Where chapter 10 promised divine shepherding and fourfold leadership, chapter 11 dramatizes the rejection of divine care. The sign-act form — in which the prophet enacts God's role as shepherd — makes the rejection intensely personal: it is not merely a bad policy decision but a repudiation of God's own pastoral engagement. The breaking of the staff 'Favor' represents what Boda calls the 'covenant collapse': when the people reject the shepherd, the protective covenant framework that sustained them disintegrates.

sections.header: Verses 12–17 — "Thirty Pieces of Silver and the Worthless Shepherd"

heb.paragraph: The 'thirty pieces of silver' (sheloshim kesef) was the compensation price for a slave gored by an ox (Exod 21:32) — the lowest possible valuation for a human life in Israelite law. When the flock 'pays' the shepherd thirty silver pieces, they assign him the value of a dead slave. God's sarcastic response — 'the handsome price at which they valued me!' — drips with bitter irony. The New Testament explicitly connects this amount to Judas's betrayal payment for Jesus (Matt 26:15; 27:3-10).

The command to 'throw it to the potter' (yotser) has generated extensive textual discussion. The MT reads 'potter' (yotser), but many scholars propose 'treasury' (ʼotsar) based on context and Syriac parallels. Matthew 27:7-10 conflates both readings: the silver was thrown into the temple (treasury) and then used to buy the potter's field. The potter may also symbolize God as the one who 'forms' and 'reshapes' (cf. Jer 18:1-6; Isa 29:16).

panels.ctx: The climax of the rejected shepherd narrative comes with the insulting payment and the rise of the worthless shepherd. The shepherd asks for his wages; the flock pays thirty pieces of silver — the price of a slave (Exod 21:32). God's furious response commands the prophet to throw this contemptuous payment 'to the potter' in the temple (11:13). The prophet then breaks the second staff, 'Union,' severing the bond between Judah and Israel. Finally, God announces that He will raise a 'foolish shepherd' (ʼozer) who will not care for the flock but will exploit it (11:15-16). This anti-shepherd is the judgment Israel brings on itself by rejecting the good shepherd: having refused God's care, they receive the leadership they deserve. The chapter closes with a woe oracle against this worthless shepherd (11:17).

cross.note: Matthew explicitly connects Judas's thirty silver pieces to Zechariah 11:12-13, interpreting the betrayal price as the fulfillment of this rejected-shepherd prophecy.

Matthew's citation of the potter's field purchase attributes the prophecy to 'Jeremiah' rather than Zechariah — a textual puzzle that may reflect the tradition of Jeremiah heading the prophetic scroll collection.
The price of thirty shekels for a slave gored by an ox is the legal background that gives the payment its insulting character: the shepherd's life is valued at the minimum rate for a dead slave.

notes.note: The thirty silver pieces and the instruction to throw them to the potter form one of the most precisely fulfilled prophecies in the New Testament. The shepherd's question — 'If you think it best, give me my pay' — is an offer of gracious separation. The flock's response — the price of a slave — reveals the depth of their contempt. God's sarcastic commentary — 'the handsome price' — exposes the insult.

The breaking of the staff 'Union' severs the final bond between Judah and Israel. The rejected shepherd's departure means not only the loss of divine protection (staff 'Favor') but the disintegration of national unity. Rejection of God's appointed leader produces both external vulnerability and internal fragmentation.

The 'foolish shepherd' raised by God is a judgment-in-kind: the people who rejected the good shepherd receive a bad one. This worthless shepherd 'will not care for the lost, or seek the young, or heal the injured, or feed the healthy' — the exact inversion of Ezekiel 34's good shepherd profile.

The woe oracle against the worthless shepherd promises that his arm will be 'completely withered' and his right eye 'totally blinded' — the instruments of power (arm) and perception (eye) rendered useless. The judgment fits the crime: a shepherd who refused to see the flock's need will lose his sight; a shepherd who refused to act will lose his strength.

Calvin saw the thirty silver pieces as the ultimate measure of human ingratitude: the divine shepherd who gave everything is valued at the price of a dead slave. The worthless shepherd of 11:15-16 represents the false leaders and false christs who arise whenever the true shepherd is rejected. Calvin applied this to the church of his own day: communities that reject faithful pastors inevitably fall under the sway of exploitative ones.

The phrase 'throw it to the potter' (hashlikhehu el hayyotser) is textually uncertain. The NET notes discuss the possibility that yotser ('potter') should be read as ʼotsar ('treasury'), which would better fit the temple setting. Matthew's account (27:5-10) appears to combine both readings: the silver is thrown into the temple and then used to purchase a potter's field.

The 'foolish shepherd' (roʼeh ʼeʼwil) is literally a 'shepherd of worthlessness.' The four things he will not do — care for the lost, seek the young, heal the injured, feed the healthy — constitute a comprehensive dereliction of pastoral duty, the precise opposite of God's shepherd-care promised in Ezekiel 34:11-16.

Boda reads the thirty silver pieces episode as the 'valuation crisis' at the heart of the shepherd allegory. The flock's contemptuous payment reveals their fundamental misapprehension of the shepherd's worth. God's instruction to throw the payment 'to the potter' in the temple may indicate that the insult is ultimately against God, not merely against the prophet-shepherd. The worthless shepherd of 11:15-17 functions as what Boda calls a 'judgment escalation': the people's rejection of the good shepherd does not leave them shepherd-less but delivers them to a destructive anti-shepherd. The woe oracle (11:17) assures that this worthless shepherd, too, will be judged.

themes.note: Chapter 11 is dominated by Judgment (the rejected shepherd, broken staffs, worthless shepherd as divine punishment), Prophecy (the sign-act form and its NT fulfillment), Covenant (the broken staffs representing annulled covenants), and Justice (the valuation crisis and the woe oracle).

rows.label: vv.1-11
rows.text: Lament over fallen glory; the prophet as rejected shepherd with two staffs, Favor and Union

rows.label: vv.12-17
rows.text: Thirty silver pieces, the potter, breaking of Union, and the rise of the worthless shepherd

lit.note: Chapter 11 is the darkest chapter of Zechariah: the good shepherd is rejected, valued at a slave's price, and replaced by a worthless shepherd — dramatizing the devastating consequences of refusing God's care.

hebtext.note: The staff named noʼam ('Favor' or 'Grace') represents God's gracious covenant with the nations on Israel's behalf. ppl.text: Chapter 11 is the darkest passage in Zechariah — a prophetic sign-act in which the prophet assumes the role of a good shepherd who is ultimately rejected by the flock he tends…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Rejected Shepherd and the Worthless Shepherd

translations.text: and said, “I will not be your shepherd. Let the dying die, and the perishing perish. Let those who are left eat one another’s flesh.”
So I said, “I will not be your shepherd. What is to die, let it die. What is to be destroyed, let it be destroyed. And let those who are left devour the flesh of one another.”

For I will no longer have pity on the people of the land,” declares the Lord. “I will give everyone into the hands of their neighbors and their king. They will devastate the land, and I will not rescue anyone from their hands.”

For I will no longer have pity on the inhabitants of this land, declares the LORD. Behold, I will cause each of them to fall into the hand of his neighbor, and each into the hand of his king, and they shall crush the land, and I will deliver none from their hand.”
